results.title: [PDF] TreeSeeker: Tree-Structured Trial, Error, and Return in Deep Search
results.content:
Third, TreeSeeker relies on external web search and browsing results, which can be noisy, in-complete, outdated, or biased. TreeSearch and TreeMem help compare evidence, preserve failure cues, and prune unproductive continuations, but they do not guarantee that all retrieved sources are reliable or that all conflicts are fully resolved. In high-stakes settings, outputs should therefore be checked against trusted sources or human expert review.
References Peter Auer, Nicolò Cesa-Bianchi, and Paul Fischer.
2002. Finite-time analysis of the multiarmed ban-dit problem. Mach. Learn., 47(2–3):235–256. [...] These results indicate that the improvement of TreeSeeker is not obtained by simply scaling up in-ference cost beyond existing strong systems. Com-pared with Flash-Searcher, TreeSeeker reduces to-tal token usage from 1474.6K to 1415.8K and re-duces tool calls from 90.20 to 71.92, while improv-ing XBench-DS performance from 50.7 to 56.3. [...] Limitations TreeSeeker has several limitations. First, our eval-uation is limited to text-based deep search bench-marks. We do not consider multimodal deep re-search settings because TreeSeeker currently does not integrate multimodal tools such as image or video understanding. Extending the framework to multimodal evidence sources is left for future work.

results.title: [Literature Review] TreeSeeker: Tree-Structured Trial, Error, and ...
results.content:
TreeSeeker addresses the "premature commitment problem" in single-path deep search by introducing two core components: TreeMem and TreeSearch.

TreeMem acts as a structured, failure-aware memory that maintains the search state. For each subgoal $g\_i$ derived from the root question, TreeMem stores a tree $T\_i$.
The structure of $T\_i$ is three-layered:

TreeSearch is the central controller for branch-and-return search. At each decision round $r$, TreeSearch performs the following steps:

Experimental Evaluation:
TreeSeeker was evaluated on XBench-DeepSearch, BrowseComp, and BrowseComp-ZH, demonstrating consistent superiority over strong open-source baselines like Flash-Searcher, IterResearch, and Tongyi-DeepSearch. [...] Deep search requires agents to answer complex questions through multi-step web interaction, a task where agents often face the challenge of deciding how to proceed when multiple search directions appear plausible but only some will lead to reliable evidence. Greedily following a promising direction can lead to extending weak continuations, while undisciplined exploration wastes budget on disconnected trials. This paper proposes TreeSeeker, an inference-time framework designed for controlled trial-and-error in deep search. TreeSeeker organizes search as branch-and-return over tree-structured states, where each branch represents a tentative direction for a subgoal. [...] `gpt-5.2`
`- w/o Textual UCB`
`- w/o Explore & Prune`
`- w/o Leaf Trace in TreeMem`

Conclusion:
TreeSeeker's branch-and-return framework, powered by TreeSearch's textual UCB control and TreeMem's structured branch states, effectively manages early-stage decision uncertainty in deep search. It complements strong reasoning and tool execution by enabling principled exploration of alternatives, exploitation of promising leads, and pruning of unproductive paths, leading to superior performance on complex deep-search tasks.

results.title: TreeSeeker: Tree-Structured Trial, Error, and Return in Deep Search
results.content:
A common failure mode for current agents in these scenarios is the "premature commitment problem." When an agent encounters multiple plausible directions for a search, it often chooses the most promising one and follows it until the computational budget is exhausted. If that initial lead turns out to be a dead end or contains misleading information, the agent may have already wasted too much effort to recover. Conversely, agents that try to explore everything without a plan often suffer from undisciplined exploration, leading to a fragmented state of knowledge that never coalesces into a final answer.

Image 1: Figure 0: Overview of the TreeSeeker framework, illustrating how search is decomposed into execution trees and managed via the TreeSearch controller. [...] While current limitations include a focus on text-only search and the inherent noise of web sources, the TreeSeeker framework provides a template for building more adaptive and resilient information-seeking systems. Future work could extend this tree-structured approach to multimodal search—incorporating images and video—or integrate it more deeply into the training phase of LLMs to create agents that are "tree-aware" from the ground up.

Flash-Searcher: Fast and Effective Web Agents via DAG-Based Parallel Execution [...] Textual UCB: Removing the semantic scoring logic led to a significant drop in performance. Without these signals, the controller tends to explore too many directions without ever committing to one, leading to budget exhaustion.
   Pruning and Returning: Disabling the PRUNE operation (and thus the ability to return from bad leads) resulted in the largest performance decline. This confirms that the ability to recognize and recover from errors is a critical component of successful deep search.
   TreeMem Structure: Removing the "leaf traces" (the recent raw history) also hurt performance, suggesting that while summaries are great for long-term memory, the agent still needs access to specific, recent details to make tactical tool decisions.

results.title: TreeSeeker: Tree-Structured Trial, Error, and Return in Deep Search
results.content:
### Community

We propose TreeSeeker, a framework for deep-search agents that explicitly models trial-and-error during long-horizon web research.

In practice, deep search is not only a reasoning problem — it is also a search-control problem. Agents often face several plausible directions early in the search process, but only some later lead to reliable evidence. Existing systems usually follow a single evolving trajectory or a fixed execution schedule, which can cause premature commitment to weak paths. [...] Deep search requires agents to answer complex questions through multi-step web search, browsing, evidence comparison, and synthesis. A central challenge is deciding how to search when several directions look plausible but only some will later lead to reliable evidence. If an agent greedily follows the current best-looking direction, it may keep extending a weak continuation. If it explores without discipline, it may waste budget on disconnected trials. We propose TreeSeeker, an inference-time framework for controlled trial-and-error in deep search. TreeSeeker organizes search as branch-and-return search over tree-structured states, where each branch is a tentative direction for a sub-goal. At each round, TreeSearch reads all sub-goal trees, identifies active goals, and uses textual UCB [...] TreeSeeker addresses this by organizing search as branch-and-return exploration over tree-structured states. Our controller, TreeSearch, uses textual signals of value, uncertainty, and risk to decide whether to exploit a promising branch, explore an uncertain alternative, or prune and return from an unproductive continuation. TreeMem keeps branch-local evidence, conflicts, and failure cues so later decisions can be informed by earlier trials.

Across XBench-DeepSearch, BrowseComp, and BrowseComp-ZH, TreeSeeker consistently outperforms strong open-source baselines. We hope this work contributes to building more reliable and adaptive research agents.
